title: ☕ Decaf: A Deconfounding Causal Generative Model

reviews.summary: This paper introduces Decaf, a deconfounding causal generative model that combines a causal normalizing flow “decoder” conditioned on latent confounders with a conditional normalizing flow “encoder” to approximate the posterior over hidden confounders, trained jointly with an ELBO. The authors provide identifiability results showing that Decaf can estimate interventional and counterfactual queries in the presence of hidden confounders when valid adjustment sets or sufficiently informative proxy variables exist, and argue that identifiable interventional queries imply identifiable counterfactual counterparts under their framework. Empirically, Decaf is evaluated on semi-synthetic benchmarks (Sachs, Ecoli70), ablations, and a fairness case study, outperforming baselines and demonstrating amortized, out-of-the-box applicability on complex graphs with multiple confounders.
strengths: Proposes a principled amalgam of causal normalizing flows (CNFs) with a conditional posterior over confounders via conditional flows, enabling amortized inference with hidden confounders.

Establishes identifiability conditions for interventional queries via valid adjustment or proxy-based arguments extended to general graphs; further connects interventional identifiability to counterfactual identifiability using a twin-SCM construction.
Provides practical masking strategies using the known graph (encoder per independent confounder depends only on its observed children) that operationalize the theory.
Evaluates on semi-synthetic variants of Sachs and Ecoli70 covering additive and non-additive regimes, with ATE and counterfactual errors and multiple baselines (ANM, CNF, DCM, Deconfounder).
Includes ablations on the number of proxies and latent dimensionality, illustrating sensitivity and validating theoretical guidance (e.g., role of completeness-like informativeness).
Uses an oracle CNF with observed confounders as a reference, contextualizing achievable performance.
Clear articulation of the model components, training objective, and inference pathways (interventional and counterfactual).
Intuitive diagrams (twin network, complex gene graph) to ground the identifiability arguments and applicability to large graphs.
Offers algorithms (in the appendix) for practitioners to check identifiability conditions of queries on a given graph.
Addresses a central practical gap: learning a single amortized generative model able to answer many queries under hidden confounding rather than training per-query models.
If the presented counterfactual identifiability result under proxy conditions holds generally, it would broaden where counterfactuals can be computed beyond causally sufficient settings.
weaknesses: Identifiability claims hinge on matching the observational evidence p(x) exactly and on completeness-style proxy assumptions that are strong, untestable from observational data alone, and may be hard to satisfy in practice.
Assumes C1-diffeomorphic structural equations and an acyclic graph; discrete variables and cycles are out of scope for the provided guarantees.
The encoder factorization and masking assume independent latent confounders and knowledge of their observed children; dependence among confounders or graph misspecification is not analyzed.

The “first time” claim for counterfactual identifiability via interventional identifiability may be overstated without a more thorough comparison to related identifiability results (e.g., counterfactual identifiability in bijective models and proximal frameworks).

Lacks baselines from proximal causal learning (e.g., proximal g-formula/bridge-function methods) that are specifically designed for proxy-based identification under hidden confounding; these are highly relevant comparisons.
No robustness study under graph misspecification, which is critical since the method relies on a provided causal graph and masking.
Limited discussion of runtime, scalability, and optimization stability (e.g., posterior collapse handling) beyond a brief note; no ablations on these practical aspects.

Fairness case study uses baselines that collapse to near-constant predictions, underplaying stronger modern fair-learning baselines, and conflates the main causal contribution with a tangential application.

The conditions on proxies (e.g., “null proxy,” independence constraints, completeness) are only sketched in the main text; it would help to state them more concretely and provide guidance on how to validate them from the graph/data.

Mechanisms ensuring the conditional flow respects the graph (masking, conditioning structure) are described at a high level; architectural details are deferred, making reproducibility harder without code.
The identifiability-check algorithms are relegated to the appendix, and the path from these checks to the “identifiable edges” highlighted in complex graphs could be more explicit in the main text.

Proximal causal learning and proxy-based identification literature beyond Wang & Blei (e.g., Tchetgen Tchetgen et al., bridge function estimators, proximal g-formula) is only lightly touched and not used as a baseline.
Recent works on counterfactual identifiability in bijective or invertible models are cited, but the novelty claim with respect to these is not fully contextualized.
Some recent deep latent proxy approaches and front-door style baselines might be relevant when applicable.
reviews.questions:
1. Could you more precisely position Proposition 6.2 relative to existing counterfactual identifiability results (e.g., for bijective models or proximal frameworks)? Under which assumptions is your result strictly novel, and can you provide a concise formal theorem in the main text?
2. In practice, how do you select the proxy and null-proxy sets (w, n) used in your identifiability conditions? Are these always children of z in your encoder, or do your identifiability-check algorithms search over broader subsets? Please clarify the operational recipe for users.
3. The encoder factorizes qφ(z|x) over independent confounders and uses only their observed children. How sensitive is Decaf to violations of independence among confounders, or to missing/extra edges in the provided graph? Can you share robustness experiments?
4. How do you enforce the graph constraints in the conditional CNF (e.g., masking and coupling-layer design) and ensure no unintended dependencies are introduced by conditioning on z?
5. What mitigation for posterior collapse did you find most effective in practice, and how sensitive are results to the KL schedule/regularization strength?
6. Could you report training/inference time, parameter counts, and memory usage for the Sachs and Ecoli70 experiments, and discuss scalability beyond 40–50 variables or more confounders?
7. Several variables in the considered datasets are discrete or mixed-type. How did you implement Decaf in those cases (e.g., dequantization, continuous relaxations), and does this affect guarantees or performance?
8. Can you include proximal causal learning baselines (e.g., bridge-function estimators) for ATE estimation on at least one setting where their assumptions hold, to better contextualize performance under proxies?
9. For Fig. 1 (Ecoli70), can you provide a reproducible mapping from the identifiability-check algorithms to the edges marked identifiable/unidentifiable, so readers can validate the classification?
10. Do you provide credible intervals or uncertainty quantification for counterfactual predictions, and if not, how might Decaf be extended to provide well-calibrated uncertainty?
reviews.assessment: This paper tackles an important and timely problem—amortized causal generative modeling under hidden confounding—and proposes a compelling solution that merges conditional normalizing flows with proxy-based identifiability theory. The claims are generally well-motivated, the methodology is sound under the stated assumptions, and the experiments on canonical graphs substantiate practical benefits over causally sufficient CGMs and a prominent deconfounding baseline. The theoretical contribution connecting interventional and counterfactual identifiability in the confounded/proxy setting is intriguing, though its novelty should be sharpened relative to existing identifiability results. The work would be strengthened by adding proximal baselines, conducting robustness studies to graph misspecification, and providing more implementation and scalability details. Overall, I find this to be a solid and valuable contribution that is likely to be of interest to the ICML community. I lean toward acceptance, conditional on clarifying the theoretical positioning and expanding the empirical comparisons where feasible.
